prompt.content:
│ │ # Storybook Setup │ ## Project Info │ | Property | Value | │ |----------|-------| │ | Version | 10.4.0-alpha.10 | │ | Renderer | @storybook/react | │ | Framework | @storybook/react-vite | │ | Builder | @storybook/builder-vite | │ | Config Dir | `.storybook` | │ | CSF Format | CSF Factory | │ | Addons | @storybook/addon-onboarding, @storybook/addon-themes, │ @storybook/addon-docs, @storybook/addon-designs, │ @storybook/addon-vitest, @storybook/addon-a11y, │ storybook-addon-pseudo-states, @chromatic-com/storybook | │ Attention: The following instructions must be followed in order to │ successfully set up Storybook in this project. Do not skip steps or │ attempt to do them out of order. │ Your goal is to make Storybook fully functional in this project by │ analyzing the codebase, │ configuring the preview with the right decorators, and writing stories │ for some components. │ The end state should be a Storybook where any component — from a small │ button to a full page — can be added without story-specific │ workarounds. All necessary providers, CSS, browser state, and network │ mocks should live in the shared preview so that just rendering the │ component in the story is enough. │ After each created story, run Vitest to verify it renders. │ If the test fails, read the error, fix the issue, and re-run until it │ passes before moving on. │ - Copy real patterns from the codebase │ - Keep the app code unchanged │ - Put the default setup in `.storybook/preview.tsx` │ - Keep app mocking and runtime setup in `.storybook/preview.tsx`, not │ in the stories │ ### Storybook Documentation Reference │ Use the following references to look up Storybook APIs, concepts, or │ examples: │ - Full docs index: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/llms.txthttps://storybook.js.org/llms.txt]8;; │ - See code snippets only with codeOnly=true param e.g. │ ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories.md?renderer=react&language=ts&codeOnly=truehttps://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories.md?renderer=react&language=ts&codeOnly=true]8;; │ Key documentation pages for this task: │ - Writing stories: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories.md?renderer=react&language=tshttps://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories...]8;; │ - Decorators: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories/decorators.md?renderer=react&language=tshttps://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories/deco...]8;; │ - Args: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories/args.md?renderer=react&language=tshttps://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories/args.md?re...]8;; │ - Play functions: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories/play-function.md?renderer=react&language=tshttps://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories/...]8;; │ - Vitest integration: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-tests/vitest-plugin.md?renderer=react&language=tshttps://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-test...]8;; │ Fetch these URLs directly when you need guidance on Storybook APIs or │ patterns. │ ### Step 1: Analyze the codebase │ Read enough of the app to understand the full runtime environment │ before writing any stories. │ Do not stop at `main.tsx` or `App.tsx`. │ Follow imports into providers, pages, hooks, and shared components │ until you know: │ - which providers exist │ - which CSS files are injected │ - which queries fetch data │ - which browser-state reads happen │ - which portals and portal roots exist │ - which pages and components show the real usage patterns │ Example of what to copy: │ ```tsx │ // src/main.tsx │ import "./index.css"; │ import App from "./App"; │ import { SessionProvider } from "./contexts/SessionContext"; │ createRoot(document.getElementById("root")!).render( │ │ │ , │ ); │ ``` │ That means Storybook should copy: │ - the `index.css` import │ - the `SessionProvider` │ - the same provider order │ Example of tracing the app deeper: │ ```tsx │ // src/App.tsx │ function App() { │ const { products, loadMoreProducts } = useProducts(); │ const { currentUser, signOut } = useSession(); │ // ... │ } │ ``` │ ```ts │ // src/hooks/useProducts.ts │ const response = await fetch(apiBaseUrl + "/products?page=1"); │ ``` │ ```ts │ // src/hooks/useTheme.ts │ const savedTheme = localStorage.getItem("theme"); │ ``` │ That means the default Storybook setup should discover and prepare: │ - provider state │ - MSW handlers for queries │ - browser-state values that are actually read during render │ ### Step 2: Build one default app environment in preview │ Set up Storybook once so most stories work without story-specific │ setup. │ Start with the smallest faithful environment: │ - the real provider tree │ - the real root CSS │ - seeded browser state if the app reads it during render │ - MSW for network/data queries │ It is fine to seed browser state such as `localStorage`, │ `sessionStorage`, and cookies when the app reads them during render. │ Seed only the specific app-owned keys and values you need. │ Do not clear all `localStorage`, `sessionStorage`, or cookies, and do │ not reset Storybook's own state. │ Do not mock or redefine the browser runtime itself. │ The stories run in Vitest browser mode, so the real browser │ environment should already exist. │ ```tsx │ // .storybook/preview.tsx │ import '../src/index.css'; // import global styles │ import MockDate from 'mockdate'; │ import { definePreview } from 'storybook/preview'; │ import { SessionProvider } from '../src/contexts/SessionContext'; │ export default definePreview({ │ decorators: [ │ (Story) => ( │ │ │ │ ), │ ], │ async beforeEach() { │ localStorage.setItem('theme', 'dark'); │ localStorage.setItem('sidebar:open', 'true'); │ MockDate.set('2024-04-01T12:00:00Z'); │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ Use this same idea for: │ - providers │ - root CSS │ - browser state │ - dates, and if the app logic depends on them during render then │ always use `mockdate` │ Example with the `mockdate` package: │ ```tsx │ import MockDate from 'mockdate'; │ import { definePreview } from 'storybook/preview'; │ export default definePreview({ │ async beforeEach() { │ MockDate.set('2024-04-01T12:00:00Z'); │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ ### Step 3: Support portals with preview-body.html │ If the app uses portals, copy that setup into Storybook too. │ Look for patterns like: │ - `createPortal(...)` │ - modal, dialog, drawer, popover, tooltip, toast, or dropdown portal │ components │ - hard-coded roots such as `#portal-root`, `#modal-root`, │ `#drawer-root`, or `#toast-root` │ Example of what to copy: │ ```tsx │ // real component │ return createPortal(, │ document.getElementById("portal-root")!); │ ``` │ That means Storybook should create the same portal root in │ `.storybook/preview-body.html`: │ ```html │ │
│ ``` │ If the app uses multiple portal roots, create all of them there: │ ```html │ │
│
│
│ ``` │ If a library portals directly to `document.body`, do not add extra │ roots for it. │ Make sure the copied page shell, CSS, and layout still allow overlays, │ fixed positioning, and z-index stacking to render correctly. │ ### Step 4: Mock side effects globally │ All network/data queries should be handled by the default Storybook │ environment. │ - Always use `msw-storybook-addon` for query mocking. │ - If you introduce MSW, run `npx msw init ./public --save` to create │ the worker file. │ - Make sure Storybook serves `./public` as a static dir so │ `mockServiceWorker.js` is available. │ - Do not mock `fetch` directly. │ - Network/data queries should return deterministic mock data. │ - If you need to change dependencies, first check the lockfile and use │ that package manager for the change. │ Example of copying a real fetch pattern into shared handlers: │ ```ts │ // real app hook │ const response = await fetch( │ apiBaseUrl + │ "/products?" + │ new URLSearchParams({ │ page: "1", │ sort: "featured", │ }), │ ); │ ``` │ ```ts │ // .storybook/msw-handlers.ts │ import { http, HttpResponse } from "msw"; │ export const mswHandlers = { │ products: [ │ http.get("]8;;https://api.example.com/products",https://api.example.com/products",]8;; │ () => │ HttpResponse.json({ │ items: [ │ { │ id: "product-1", │ name: "Example product", │ description: "Mock product description", │ imageUrl: │ "]8;;https://images.example.com/product.jpg",https://images.example.com/product.jpg",]8;; │ price: 42, │ }, │ ], │ }), │ ), │ ], │ }; │ ``` │ ```tsx │ // .storybook/preview.tsx │ import { definePreview } from 'storybook/preview'; │ import { initialize, mswLoader } from 'msw-storybook-addon'; │ import { mswHandlers } from './msw-handlers'; │ initialize({ │ onUnhandledRequest: 'bypass', │ }); │ export default definePreview({ │ loaders: [mswLoader], │ parameters: { │ msw: { │ handlers: mswHandlers, │ }, │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ ```ts │ // .storybook/main.ts │ import type { StorybookConfig } from "@storybook/react-vite"; │ const config: StorybookConfig = { │ staticDirs: ["../public"], │ }; │ export default config; │ ``` │ Keep these mocks global. │ Do not put fetch mocks in individual stories. │ Only add handlers for requests that the shared preview setup or the │ stories actually use. │ Do not add catch-all handlers that can hide unrelated failures. │ If the defaults are not enough, improve the shared default setup │ instead. │ Seed browser state when needed, but do not mock `window`, `document`, │ `navigator`, observers, or similar runtime APIs. │ The only exception is `mockdate` when date-based rendering exists. │ ### Step 5: Write stories │ Try to find around 10 good candidate components for story files. │ Write colocated stories for top-level components, from low-level │ reusable components up to page components. │ Write up to 10 story files, or fewer only if the codebase clearly has │ fewer meaningful targets. │ The stories should use JSX copied from real usage patterns in: │ - pages │ - app shells │ - routes │ - tests │ - existing feature code │ As a rule of thumb, each story file should have around 3 story exports │ when the component or page has enough meaningful states. │ It can have more when the real usage supports it, up to 10 story │ exports in one file. │ Always show all imports explicitly in story and preview files. │ Do not rely on omitted or implied imports in examples or generated │ code. │ #### Story tags │ Every story meta must include the `ai-generated` tag to identify │ AI-created stories: │ ```tsx │ import preview from '#.storybook/preview'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { SomeComponent } from './SomeComponent'; │ const meta = preview.meta({ │ component: SomeComponent, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ }); │ export const Default = meta.story({ │ render: () => , │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button')).toBeVisible(); │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ If a story could not be fully fixed after the self-healing loop (the │ test still fails │ or the rendering is incomplete), add the `needs-work` tag alongside │ `ai-generated`: │ ```ts │ const meta = preview.meta({ │ component: SomeComponent, │ tags: ['ai-generated', 'needs-work'], │ }); │ ``` │ #### Args vs render │ For simple components where props drive the state, prefer `args` │ stories — no `render` function needed: │ ```tsx │ import preview from '#.storybook/preview'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { Button } from './Button'; │ const meta = preview.meta({ │ component: Button, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ }); │ export const Primary = meta.story({ │ args: { │ variant: 'primary', │ children: 'Save', │ }, │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button', { name: /save/i │ })).toBeVisible(); │ }, │ }); │ export const Disabled = meta.story({ │ args: { │ variant: 'primary', │ disabled: true, │ children: 'Save', │ }, │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button')).toBeDisabled(); │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ Use `render` when the story needs composition — wrapping the component │ in layout, combining multiple components, or passing children as JSX: │ ```tsx │ import preview from '#.storybook/preview'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { Button } from './Button'; │ import { Card } from './Card'; │ const meta = preview.meta({ │ component: Button, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ }); │ export const InsideCard = meta.story({ │ render: () => ( │ │ │ │ ), │ play: async ({ canvas, userEvent }) => { │ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button', { name: /save/i │ })).toBeVisible(); │ await userEvent.click(canvas.getByRole('button', { name: /save/i })); │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ Keep app mocking and runtime setup in preview, not in the stories. │ Do not build large story-specific harnesses. │ Do not write story files for subcomponents, hooks, contexts, or │ helpers. │ Do not create new application components. │ Do not add a custom `title`. │ Do not stop after only a few easy targets if the codebase has more │ meaningful components or pages available. │ ### Step 6: Write a play function for every story │ Every named story export must have a `play` function. │ The `play` function is not optional, even for simple stories. │ The purpose of the `play` function is to prove that the story actually │ works in the copied Storybook environment: │ - the story renders something real and non-empty │ - the decorators provide the needed context │ - the CSS is applied well enough for the intended state to be visible │ - the MSW mocks or seeded browser state are actually being used │ - important interactions, async loading states, and portals behave │ correctly │ Use `play` functions to verify behavior, not just to click around. │ A story without assertions is incomplete. │ Use tools from `storybook/test` such as: │ - `expect` │ - `waitFor` │ Prefer `canvas` and `userEvent` from the `play` context. │ Do not destructure `canvasElement` just to create `const canvas = │ within(canvasElement)`. │ Do not import `userEvent` from `storybook/test`; use `userEvent` from │ the `play` context instead. │ Only use `canvasElement.ownerDocument` when you need to query outside │ the canvas, such as for portals. │ Example: │ ```tsx │ import type { StoryObj } from "@storybook/react-vite"; │ export const FilledForm: Story = { │ play: async ({ canvas, userEvent }) => { │ const emailInput = canvas.getByLabelText("email", { │ selector: "input", │ }); │ await userEvent.type(emailInput, "example-email@email.com", { │ delay: 100, │ }); │ const passwordInput = canvas.getByLabelText("password", { │ selector: "input", │ }); │ await userEvent.type(passwordInput, "ExamplePassword", { │ delay: 100, │ }); │ const submitButton = canvas.getByRole("button"); │ await userEvent.click(submitButton); │ }, │ }; │ ``` │ The assertions should match the real pattern you copied: │ - for provider-backed stories, assert the provider-dependent UI │ appears correctly │ - for mocked-data stories, wait for the mocked data to appear and │ assert on it │ - for CSS-sensitive states, assert on visibility, text layout, │ class-driven states, or meaningful computed styles │ - for routing or navigation stories, assert the routed state or │ navigation outcome │ - for portal stories, query from `canvasElement.ownerDocument` when │ the UI renders outside the canvas │ Examples of useful checks: │ - a themed button has the expected label and is visibly enabled or │ disabled │ - a modal opened through a decorator or provider is visible in the │ portal root │ - mocked API data appears in the page instead of a loading spinner │ forever │ - a selected tab actually shows the selected panel │ - a toast, alert, or badge has the expected accessible text and visual │ state │ - a CSS class or computed style confirms the real state that matters │ ### Step 7: Prove CSS is loaded in exactly one story named `CssCheck` │ In exactly one story, named `CssCheck`, assert a component-specific │ computed style. `toBeVisible` passes on an unstyled component; a │ concrete style value proves the shared preview loaded the app's CSS. │ Pick a visually distinctive component, read a styling value from its │ source, and assert it with `getComputedStyle`: │ ```tsx │ export const CssCheck: Story = { │ args: { children: "Submit" }, │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ const button = canvas.getByRole("button", { name: /submit/i }); │ // PrimaryButton uses bg-blue-600 — fails if Tailwind / global CSS did │ not load. │ await expect(getComputedStyle(button).backgroundColor).toBe("rgb(37, │ 99, 235)"); │ }, │ }; │ ``` │ ### Step 8: Cover the patterns you found │ Write stories for the real patterns in the codebase, for example: │ - a low-level reusable component in real JSX usage │ - a provider-backed component │ - a browser-state-backed component │ - a fetched-data component │ - a real page component │ Use `App.tsx` to inspect the real provider tree and usage patterns, │ but do not make a story for `App` when the codebase has actual page │ components. │ Example page story: │ ```tsx │ import preview from '#.storybook/preview'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { ProductPage } from './ProductPage'; │ const meta = preview.meta({ │ component: ProductPage, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ }); │ export const Default = meta.story({ │ render: () => , │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect( │ canvas.getByRole('heading', { name: /products/i }), │ ).toBeVisible(); │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ ### Step 9: Verify both rendering and types │ As you work, verify the stories with Vitest: │ ```bash │ npx vitest --project storybook │ ``` │ Also verify types so you catch missing required props, broken imports, │ and preview typing issues. Run the same TypeScript command the project │ itself uses. │ ```bash │ │ ``` │ After verification passes, review every changed file and remove │ anything that is not needed for the final solution, especially debug │ fixes, overly broad mocks, unnecessary dependencies, and eval │ artifacts. │ Keep iterating until: │ - every story you wrote passes │ - every story you wrote has a meaningful passing `play` function │ - the changed stories and preview setup pass the project's real │ TypeScript check │ - the rendered output looks sensible │ - the default global mocked environment is strong enough that stories │ do not need manual fetch overrides │ - stories no longer fail because the shared preview setup and story │ JSX are fixed │ - all passing stories have `tags: ['ai-generated']` in their meta │ - any stories that still need work have `tags: ['ai-generated', │ 'needs-work']` in their meta

content.thinking: Let me start by analyzing the codebase to understand the project structure, providers, CSS, data fetching patterns, and components.

Explore this project thoroughly. I need to understand:

1. The project structure - what's in src/, what directories exist
2. The entry point (main.tsx or index.tsx) - what providers, CSS imports, and setup it does
3. App.tsx - the provider tree, routing, layout
4. All providers/contexts used
5. All CSS files imported at the root level
6. Data fetching patterns (fetch, axios, react-query, etc.)
7. Browser state usage (localStorage, sessionStorage, cookies)
8. Portal usage (createPortal, modal roots, etc.)
9. Key components and pages that would be good story candidates
10. The package.json for dependencies and scripts
11. The .storybook directory - what's already configured
12. Any existing stories
13. tsconfig for understanding path aliases

content.text:
Perfect! Now let me compile my comprehensive findings into a report.

## Comprehensive Project Exploration Report

I have thoroughly explored the Storybook eval project. Here's a detailed breakdown of all the key information:

---

## 1. PROJECT STRUCTURE

**Root Directory**: `/Users/yannbraga/open-source/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-28T22-04-26Z-1cc21fae/project/packages/lib`

**Main Directories**:
- `src/` - Application source code
  - `components/` - Reusable UI components
  - `context/` - React Context providers
  - `gql/` - GraphQL queries, mutations, and generated types
  - `hooks/` - Custom React hooks
  - `pages/` - Page components
  - `stories/` - Storybook demo stories
  - `test_utils/` - Test utilities and test wrappers
  - `types/` - TypeScript type definitions
  - `utils/` - Utility functions
  - `constants/` - App constants
  - `analytics/` - Analytics integration
- `.storybook/` - Storybook configuration
- `config/` - Configuration files
- `vitest.config.ts` - Vitest testing configuration

---

## 2. ENTRY POINT & PROVIDERS

**Storybook Configuration**:
- **File**: `.storybook/preview.tsx` 
- **Setup**: Minimal config with controls matchers for color, date, and a11y testing setup
- **Main Config**: `.storybook/main.ts` defines story patterns and addons

**Key Providers Used** (found in context setup):

1. **AuthProvider** (`src/context/AuthProvider/index.tsx`)
   - Manages authentication state (isAuthenticated, hasCheckedAuth)
   - Supports local auth and remote auth strategies
   - Methods: `localLogin()`, `logoutAndRedirect()`, `dispatchAuthenticated()`
   - Uses cookies (js-cookie) for staging environment tracking
   - Makes authenticated GraphQL queries to verify user status

2. **ToastProvider** (`src/context/toast/index.tsx`)
   - Wraps LeafyGreen UI's ToastProvider
   - Provides toast methods: `success()`, `warning()`, `error()`, `info()`, `progress()`
   - Handles toast timeout and dismissal
   - Uses portals for toast display

3. **LGToastProvider** (LeafyGreen)
   - Parent provider for toast system

---

## 3. APP.TSX STRUCTURE

The project uses a library structure rather than a full app. Key architectural patterns:

- **No main App.tsx** - This is a component library (ui-lib)
- **Entry via Storybook** - Stories are the primary entry point
- **Component-based** - Each component is independently tested and showcased
- **Router Integration**: Uses react-router-dom (v6.18.0) for ProtectedRoute and LoginPage
- **Error Handling**: ErrorBoundary component that switches between Sentry and default error boundaries

---

## 4. ALL PROVIDERS/CONTEXTS USED

1. **AuthProvider** - Authentication state management
   - Context: `AuthStateContext`
   - Hook: `useAuthProviderContext()`

2. **ToastContext** - Toast notifications
   - Context: `ToastContext`
   - Hook: `useToastContext()`

3. **Apollo Client** - GraphQL data layer
   - Dependency: `@apollo/client@4.1.3`
   - Used with MockedProvider for testing
   - Custom test utilities included

4. **LeafyGreen UI Theme/Components**
   - Multiple component libraries imported
   - Uses emotion styling

5. **Sentry** - Error reporting
   - `@sentry/react@10.31.0`, `@sentry/core@10.31.0`
   - Initialized conditionally in production
   - Breadcrumb tracking for debugging

---

## 5. CSS FILES IMPORTED AT ROOT LEVEL

**CSS Strategy**: Emotion-based styled-components (CSS-in-JS)

**CSS Files Found**:
- `/src/stories/page.css`
- `/src/stories/button.css`
- `/src/stories/header.css`

**Global Styles** (`src/components/styles/globalStyles.ts`):
- `resetStyles` - Box-sizing reset following LeafyGreen recommendations
- `bodyStyles` - Font family and size using LeafyGreen tokens

**Emotion Usage**:
- Direct `styled()` components throughout
- CSS helper from `@emotion/react`
- LeafyGreen emotion tokens package

---

## 6. DATA FETCHING PATTERNS

**Apollo Client (GraphQL)**:
- Primary data fetching method
- Hooks: `useQuery<QueryType, QueryVariables>(QUERY_DOCUMENT)`
- Examples:
  - `useAdminBetaFeatures()` - Admin beta feature settings
  - `useUserBetaFeatures()` - User beta feature settings
  - `useMergedBetaFeatures()` - Merged admin + user features
- Query documents: GraphQL files in `src/gql/queries/`
- Mutation support: `src/gql/mutations/`

**Fetch API** (Direct):
- Used in `AuthProvider` for authentication endpoints
- `fetchWithRetry()` utility function in `src/utils/request/`
- Headers include staging environment support

**No React Query/TanStack Query** - Uses Apollo Client exclusively

---

## 7. BROWSER STATE USAGE

**localStorage**:
- `getLocalStorageString()`, `setLocalStorageString()` - Generic string storage
- `getLocalStorageBoolean()`, `setLocalStorageBoolean()` - Boolean storage with try-catch
- Usage:
  - **Pagination**: `RECENT_PAGE_SIZE_KEY` - Stores user's preferred page size
  - **User ID**: Retrieved in observability setup
  - **Error handling**: Silent failures if localStorage unavailable

**Cookies**:
- `js-cookie@3.0.5` library
- Usage: `evg-staging-environment` cookie for routing to personal staging environments
- Set with 365-day expiration

**sessionStorage**: No usage found

**Environment Variables**:
- Utilities in `src/utils/environmentVariables/`
- Functions: `getUserStagingKey()`, `isStaging()`, `getUserStagingHeader()`

---

## 8. PORTAL USAGE

**Toast Portals**:
- `ToastProvider` accepts `portalClassName` prop
- Passed to LeafyGreen's `LGToastProvider`
- Creates separate DOM node for toast notifications

**No createPortal direct usage** found in component code - delegation to LeafyGreen UI

---

## 9. KEY COMPONENTS FOR STORY CANDIDATES

**Already Has Stories**:
- `Button` (Button.stories.ts)
- `Header` (Header.stories.ts)
- `Page` (Page.stories.ts)

**Component Library** (Good story candidates):

1. **Badge Components**:
   - `TaskStatusBadge` - Shows task status with conditional styling
   - `TestStatusBadge` - Test result status display

2. **Icon System**:
   - `Icon` / `createIconComponent` wrapper
   - `AnimatedIcon` - SVG icons with pause/unpause on hover

3. **Table Components**:
   - `BaseTable` - Main table component
   - `TableControl`, `TableLoader`, `TablePlaceholder`, `TablePopover` - Subcomponents
   - Uses TanStack Table Core v8.20.5
   - Supports virtual scrolling via LeafyGreen

4. **Form Components**:
   - `TextInputWithGlyph` - Input with icon
   - `TreeSelect` - Tree-based select component
   - `Popconfirm` - Confirmation popover with custom buttons

5. **Pagination**:
   - `Pagination` component
   - `PageSizeSelector` component
   - `usePagination` hook for state management

6. **Other Notable Components**:
   - `Accordion` - Expandable content
   - `ErrorBoundary` - Error handling wrapper
   - `FullPageLoad` - Loading skeleton
   - `IconWithTooltip` - Icon + tooltip combo
   - `ExpiringAnnouncementTooltip` - Time-limited announcements
   - `ProtectedRoute` - Auth-protected routing

7. **Pages**:
   - `LoginPage` - Authentication page with local/remote auth support

---

## 10. PACKAGE.JSON DETAILS

**Key Dependencies**:
- **React**: 18.3.1
- **React DOM**: 18.3.1
- **Router**: react-router-dom 6.18.0
- **GraphQL**: @apollo/client 4.1.3, graphql 16.12.0
- **UI Library**: @leafygreen-ui/* (comprehensive suite)
  - button, badge, checkbox, table, pagination, select, etc.
  - Styling: @emotion/react 11.14.0, @emotion/styled 11.14.1
- **Error Tracking**: @sentry/react 10.31.0, @sentry/core 10.31.0
- **Observability**: @honeycombio/opentelemetry-web 1.1.0, @opentelemetry/* packages
- **Utilities**: 
  - date-fns 4.1.0
  - js-cookie 3.0.5
  - query-string 9.1.1
  - @tanstack/table-core 8.20.5

**Dev Dependencies**:
- **Testing**: @testing-library/react 16.3.0, @testing-library/user-event 14.6.1
- **Storybook**: @storybook/react-vite, chromatic 13.3.4
- **Build**: vite 7.3.1, vitest 4.1.2
- **Code Quality**: eslint 9.39.1, prettier 3.7.4
- **Types**: TypeScript ~5.9.3

**Scripts**:
- `npm run storybook` - Start dev server (port 6008)
- `npm run storybook:build` / `build-storybook` - Build static storybook
- `npm test` - Run vitest
- `npm run vitest:storybook` - Run Storybook vitest addon tests
- `npm run chromatic` - Visual regression testing
- `npm run codegen` - Generate GraphQL types
- `npm run check-types` - TypeScript type checking

---

## 11. STORYBOOK CONFIGURATION

**.storybook/main.ts**:
```
- Stories pattern: '../src/**/*.mdx', '../src/**/*.stories.@(js|jsx|mjs|ts|tsx)'
- Addons:
  - @chromatic-com/storybook (visual regression)
  - @storybook/addon-vitest (test integration)
  - @storybook/addon-a11y (accessibility)
  - @storybook/addon-docs (documentation)
  - @storybook/addon-onboarding
- Framework: @storybook/react-vite
```

**.storybook/preview.tsx**:
```
- Control matchers for color and date fields
- a11y test marked as 'todo'
```

**Support Files**:
- `eval-support/transcript.tsx` & `.mdx` - Evaluation transcript
- `eval-support/summary.mdx` - Summary page
- `eval-results/data.json` - Evaluation data

---

## 12. EXISTING STORIES

**Found Stories**:
1. `/src/stories/Button.stories.ts` - Example button component with variants
2. `/src/stories/Header.stories.ts` - Header component stories
3. `/src/stories/Page.stories.ts` - Page layout stories
4. `/src/stories/Configure.mdx` - Storybook setup documentation

**Story Format**: Modern CSF3 with Meta/StoryObj types

---

## 13. TSCONFIG CONFIGURATION

**tsconfig.json**:
```json
- Extends: ../../tsconfig.base.json
- Path aliases: "*": ["./src/*"]
- Includes:
  - src/**
  - test_utils/index.tsx
  - test_utils/custom-queries.ts
  - .storybook/preview.tsx
  - src/**/*.d.ts
  - vitest.d.ts
- References: tsconfig.node.json for build tools
```

**Key Setup**:
- Path alias allows importing from `@/` to `src/`
- No strict path prefixes required
- Compiles test utilities alongside main code

---

## 14. TESTING SETUP

**Framework**: Vitest 4.1.2
**Config**: `vitest.config.ts`

**Test Environment**:
- jsdom for DOM testing
- Globals enabled (describe, it, etc. without imports)
- Setup file: `config/vitest/setupTests.ts`
- Global setup: `config/vitest/global-setup.ts`

**Setup File Includes**:
- `@testing-library/jest-dom` matchers
- `vitest-canvas-mock` for canvas elements
- jest compatibility polyfill for user-event
- HTMLElement popover API mocks

**Projects**:
1. Default project - jsdom environment
2. Storybook project - Browser-based testing with Playwright
   - Headless Chromium
   - Optional mobile device emulation (iPhone 17_0)
   - Device scale factor: 2x

**Test Utilities** (`src/test_utils/index.tsx`):
- `customRender()` / `render()` - Enhanced testing-library render
- `renderWithRouterMatch()` - Router + render combo
- `renderComponentWithHook()` - Hook testing utility
- `customScreen`/`screen` - Enhanced query selectors
- `createWrapper()` - Generic wrapper factory
- Custom queries via `custom-queries.ts`
- MockedProvider + MockedResponse for Apollo
- Exports: act, fireEvent, userEvent, waitFor, within, stubGetClientRects

---

## 15. ADDITIONAL INFRASTRUCTURE

**Error Reporting** (`src/utils/errorReporting/index.ts`):
- `reportError()` - Reports to Sentry with severity levels (severe/warning)
- `leaveBreadcrumb()` - Timeline logging for debugging
- `initializeErrorHandling()` - Conditional Sentry initialization
- Fallback to console logging if Sentry not initialized

**Observability** (`src/utils/observability/honeycomb.ts`):
- Honeycomb integration for tracing
- User ID tracking via localStorage
- OpenTelemetry instrumentation

**Request Utilities** (`src/utils/request/`):
- `fetchWithRetry()` - Fetch wrapper with retry logic
- `getUserStagingHeader()` - Headers for staging environment routing

**Hooks Library**:
- `useBetaFeatures()` - Beta feature flags (admin + user merged)
- `useQueryParam()` - URL query parameter state
- `usePagination()` - Pagination state with localStorage + query params
- `useErrorToast()` - Error notification helper
- `useKeyboardShortcut()` - Keyboard event handling
- `useOnClickOutside()` - Click outside detection
- `usePageTitle()` - Document title management
- `useDebouncedCallback()` - Debounced function wrapper
- `usePrevious()` - Previous value reference

---

## Summary of Key Architectural Patterns

1. **Component Library Architecture** - Focuses on reusable, well-tested UI components
2. **Emotion Styling** - CSS-in-JS approach using emotion + LeafyGreen design system
3. **Apollo Client** - GraphQL-first data management with comprehensive testing mocks
4. **Context + Hooks** - React Context for global state (Auth, Toast) + custom hooks for logic
5. **Comprehensive Testing** - Vitest + Storybook addon + Testing Library + Playwright
6. **Error Management** - Sentry for production, console for development
7. **Observability-First** - Honeycomb tracing, OpenTelemetry instrumentation
8. **Accessibility** - Storybook a11y addon, LeafyGreen accessible components
9. **Type Safety** - TypeScript with strict configuration, GraphQL codegen

---

All absolute file paths relevant to this exploration are documented above. The project is well-structured for component documentation via Storybook with comprehensive testing infrastructure.

  - @storybook/addon-onboarding
- Framework: @storybook/react-vite
```

**.storybook/preview.tsx**:
```
- Control matchers for color and date fields
- a11y test marked as 'todo'
```

**Support Files**:
- `eval-support/transcript.tsx` & `.mdx` - Evaluation transcript
- `eval-support/summary.mdx` - Summary page
- `eval-results/data.json` - Evaluation data

---

## 12. EXISTING STORIES

**Found Stories**:
1. `/src/stories/Button.stories.ts` - Example button component with variants
2. `/src/stories/Header.stories.ts` - Header component stories
3. `/src/stories/Page.stories.ts` - Page layout stories
4. `/src/stories/Configure.mdx` - Storybook setup documentation

**Story Format**: Modern CSF3 with Meta/StoryObj types

---

## 13. TSCONFIG CONFIGURATION

**tsconfig.json**:
```json
- Extends: ../../tsconfig.base.json
- Path aliases: "*": ["./src/*"]
- Includes:
  - src/**
  - test_utils/index.tsx
  - test_utils/custom-queries.ts
  - .storybook/preview.tsx
  - src/**/*.d.ts
  - vitest.d.ts
- References: tsconfig.node.json for build tools
```

**Key Setup**:
- Path alias allows importing from `@/` to `src/`
- No strict path prefixes required
- Compiles test utilities alongside main code

---

## 14. TESTING SETUP

**Framework**: Vitest 4.1.2
**Config**: `vitest.config.ts`

**Test Environment**:
- jsdom for DOM testing
- Globals enabled (describe, it, etc. without imports)
- Setup file: `config/vitest/setupTests.ts`
- Global setup: `config/vitest/global-setup.ts`

**Setup File Includes**:
- `@testing-library/jest-dom` matchers
- `vitest-canvas-mock` for canvas elements
- jest compatibility polyfill for user-event
- HTMLElement popover API mocks

**Projects**:
1. Default project - jsdom environment
2. Storybook project - Browser-based testing with Playwright
   - Headless Chromium
   - Optional mobile device emulation (iPhone 17_0)
   - Device scale factor: 2x

**Test Utilities** (`src/test_utils/index.tsx`):
- `customRender()` / `render()` - Enhanced testing-library render
- `renderWithRouterMatch()` - Router + render combo
- `renderComponentWithHook()` - Hook testing utility
- `customScreen`/`screen` - Enhanced query selectors
- `createWrapper()` - Generic wrapper factory
- Custom queries via `custom-queries.ts`
- MockedProvider + MockedResponse for Apollo
- Exports: act, fireEvent, userEvent, waitFor, within, stubGetClientRects

---

## 15. ADDITIONAL INFRASTRUCTURE

**Error Reporting** (`src/utils/errorReporting/index.ts`):
- `reportError()` - Reports to Sentry with severity levels (severe/warning)
- `leaveBreadcrumb()` - Timeline logging for debugging
- `initializeErrorHandling()` - Conditional Sentry initialization
- Fallback to console logging if Sentry not initialized

**Observability** (`src/utils/observability/honeycomb.ts`):
- Honeycomb integration for tracing
- User ID tracking via localStorage
- OpenTelemetry instrumentation

**Request Utilities** (`src/utils/request/`):
- `fetchWithRetry()` - Fetch wrapper with retry logic
- `getUserStagingHeader()` - Headers for staging environment routing

**Hooks Library**:
- `useBetaFeatures()` - Beta feature flags (admin + user merged)
- `useQueryParam()` - URL query parameter state
- `usePagination()` - Pagination state with localStorage + query params
- `useErrorToast()` - Error notification helper
- `useKeyboardShortcut()` - Keyboard event handling
- `useOnClickOutside()` - Click outside detection
- `usePageTitle()` - Document title management
- `useDebouncedCallback()` - Debounced function wrapper
- `usePrevious()` - Previous value reference

---

## Summary of Key Architectural Patterns

1. **Component Library Architecture** - Focuses on reusable, well-tested UI components
2. **Emotion Styling** - CSS-in-JS approach using emotion + LeafyGreen design system
3. **Apollo Client** - GraphQL-first data management with comprehensive testing mocks
4. **Context + Hooks** - React Context for global state (Auth, Toast) + custom hooks for logic
5. **Comprehensive Testing** - Vitest + Storybook addon + Testing Library + Playwright
6. **Error Management** - Sentry for production, console for development
7. **Observability-First** - Honeycomb tracing, OpenTelemetry instrumentation
8. **Accessibility** - Storybook a11y addon, LeafyGreen accessible components
9. **Type Safety** - TypeScript with strict configuration, GraphQL codegen

---

All absolute file paths relevant to this exploration are documented above. The project is well-structured for component documentation via Storybook with comprehensive testing infrastructure.

Perfect! Now I have all the information I need. Let me create a comprehensive report:

## PROJECT STRUCTURE AND COMPONENT ANALYSIS REPORT

Based on my complete exploration of `/Users/yannbraga/open-source/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-28T22-04-26Z-1cc21fae/project/packages/lib`, here's a comprehensive breakdown:

---

## 1. COMPONENT DIRECTORY STRUCTURE

**All components located at:** `/Users/yannbraga/open-source/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-28T22-04-26Z-1cc21fae/project/packages/lib/src/components/`

### Component Directories:
- `Accordion/` - Expandable/collapsible container with animation support
- `Badge/` - Badge components (TaskStatusBadge, TestStatusBadge)
- `ErrorBoundary/` - Error boundary wrapper with Sentry integration
- `ExpiringAnnouncementTooltip/` - Time-limited announcement with cookie management
- `FullPageLoad/` - Full-screen loading indicator
- `Icon/` - Icon system with custom icons and animation support
- `IconWithTooltip/` - Icon with integrated tooltip
- `PageSizeSelector/` - Dropdown to select items per page
- `Pagination/` - Page navigation component
- `Popconfirm/` - Confirmation dialog built on Tooltip
- `ProtectedRoute/` - Route guard for authenticated users
- `Table/` - Complex table component with filtering and search
- `TextInputWithGlyph/` - Text input with icon and persistent placeholder
- `TreeSelect/` - Hierarchical checkbox selector
- `styles/` - Global and component styles

---

## 2. PAGES

**Location:** `/Users/yannbraga/open-source/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-28T22-04-26Z-1cc21fae/project/packages/lib/src/pages/`

- **LoginPage** (`index.tsx`) - Local auth form with username/password fields, redirects authenticated users

---

## 3. CONTEXT PROVIDERS

### AuthProvider
**Path:** `/Users/yannbraga/open-source/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-28T22-04-26Z-1cc21fae/project/packages/lib/src/context/AuthProvider/index.tsx`

**Key features:**
- Supports both local and remote authentication strategies
- Methods: `localLogin()`, `logoutAndRedirect()`, `dispatchAuthenticated()`
- State: `isAuthenticated`, `hasCheckedAuth`
- Makes GraphQL query to check authentication on mount
- Uses `fetchWithRetry` with staging environment support

### Toast Provider
**Path:** `/Users/yannbraga/open-source/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-28T22-04-26Z-1cc21fae/project/packages/lib/src/context/toast/index.tsx`

**Key features:**
- Wraps LeafyGreen Toast provider
- Methods: `success()`, `error()`, `warning()`, `info()`, `progress()`
- Accepts options: `closable`, `shouldTimeout`, `title`, `timeout`, `onClose`
- Uses LeafyGreen `Variant` enum for styling

---

## 4. DETAILED COMPONENT BREAKDOWN

### Accordion
**Path:** `/Users/yannbraga/open-source/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-28T22-04-26Z-1cc21fae/project/packages/lib/src/components/Accordion/index.tsx`

**Props:**
- `title`: Required, display text
- `children`: Content to show/hide
- `open?: boolean` - Controlled state
- `defaultOpen?: boolean` - Initial uncontrolled state
- `onToggle?: ({ isVisible }) => void`
- `toggledTitle?: React.ReactNode` - Alternative title when open
- `subtitle?: React.ReactNode`
- `caretIcon?: 'Caret' | 'Chevron'` (default: Chevron)
- `caretAlign?: 'start' | 'center' | 'end'` (default: center)
- `titleTag?: React.FC` - HTML tag for title
- `useIndent?: boolean` (default: true)
- `disableAnimations?: boolean` (default: true)
- `data-cy?: string`

**Features:** Grid-based animation, indented content, subtitle support

---

### Badge Components
**Path:** `/Users/yannbraga/open-source/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-28T22-04-26Z-1cc21fae/project/packages/lib/src/components/Badge/`

**TaskStatusBadge** (`TaskStatusBadge/index.tsx`)
- Props: `status: TaskStatus | TaskStatusUmbrella`, `taskCount?: number`
- Maps statuses: Inactive, Unstarted, Blocked, Pending, Started, Dispatched, Failed, Succeeded, etc.
- Custom colors for: SetupFailed (purple), SystemFailed (dark purple), KnownIssue (red outline)
- Uses LeafyGreen Badge variants

**TestStatusBadge** (`TestStatusBadge/index.tsx`)
- Props: `status?: string | null`
- Maps: Pass (green), Fail (red), Skip (yellow), SilentFail (blue)

---

### BaseTable
**Path:** `/Users/yannbraga/open-source/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-28T22-04-26Z-1cc21fae/project/packages/lib/src/components/Table/BaseTable.tsx`

**Props:**
- `table: LeafyGreenVirtualTable<T> | LeafyGreenTable<T>` - Required
- `data-cy-table?: string`
- `data-cy-row?: string`
- `loading?: boolean`
- `loadingRows?: number` (default: 5)
- `numTotalItems?: number`
- `selectedRowIndexes?: number[]` (highlights with blue tint)
- `disabledRowIndexes?: number[]`
- `usePagination?: boolean` (default: false)
- `emptyComponent?: React.ReactNode` (default: "No data to display")
- `rowCss?: SerializedStyles`

**Features:**
- Supports virtual scrolling
- Pagination support
- Column filtering (TreeSelect or search)
- Row selection and disabled states
- Sticky header for virtual rows
- Expanded content support

**Column Meta Configuration:**
```typescript
meta?: {
  search?: { "data-cy"?: string; placeholder?: string };
  treeSelect?: { "data-cy"?: string; filterOptions?: boolean; options: TreeDataEntry[] };
  width?: string;
}
```

---

### ErrorBoundary
**Path:** `/Users/yannbraga/open-source/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-28T22-04-26Z-1cc21fae/project/packages/lib/src/components/ErrorBoundary/index.tsx`

**Props:**
- `children: React.ReactNode`
- `homeURL: string` - URL to show in error fallback

**Files:**
- `DefaultErrorBoundary.tsx` - Used when Sentry not initialized
- `ErrorFallback/ErrorFallback.tsx` - Error UI with "Back To Home" link
- `SentryErrorBoundary.tsx` - Used when Sentry initialized

---

### ExpiringAnnouncementTooltip
**Path:** `/Users/yannbraga/open-source/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-28T22-04-26Z-1cc21fae/project/packages/lib/src/components/ExpiringAnnouncementTooltip/index.tsx`

**Props:**
- `cookieName: string` - Cookie to track dismissal
- `children: React.ReactNode` - Tooltip content
- `title: React.ReactNode` - Tooltip title
- `activeDays?: number` (default: 8) - Days to show icon after dismissal
- `loading?: boolean` (default: false)
- `tooltipAlign?: GuideCueProps['tooltipAlign']`

**Features:** Opens automatically on first view, uses cookies for persistence

---

### FullPageLoad
**Path:** `/Users/yannbraga/open-source/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-28T22-04-26Z-1cc21fae/project/packages/lib/src/components/FullPageLoad/index.tsx`

Simple fullscreen loader component. No props, renders "LOADING..." text centered.

---

### Icon/AnimatedIcon
**Path:** `/Users/yannbraga/open-source/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-28T22-04-26Z-1cc21fae/project/packages/lib/src/components/Icon/`

**Icon Component** (Default export from `index.ts`)
- Built with LeafyGreen's `createIconComponent()`
- Accepts `glyph` prop with icon name
- Exported glyphs include: EvergreenLogo, ParsleyLogo, KnownFailure, ArrowWithCircle, ClosedEye, Expand, Ignored, GitHub, and all LeafyGreen glyphs

**AnimatedIcon** (`AnimatedIcon/index.tsx`)
- Props: `icon: ForwardRefExoticComponent`, `alwaysAnimate?: boolean` (default: false)
- Pauses/unpauses SVG animations on mouse enter/leave
- Checks for animate, animateTransform, animateMotion elements

---

### IconWithTooltip
**Path:** `/Users/yannbraga/open-source/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-28T22-04-26Z-1cc21fae/project/packages/lib/src/components/IconWithTooltip/index.tsx`

**Props:** All Icon props + `data-cy?: string`, `children: React.ReactNode` (tooltip content)

**Features:** Tooltip triggers on hover, aligned to top-middle, max-width 300px

---

### LoginPage
**Path:** `/Users/yannbraga/open-source/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-28T22-04-26Z-1cc21fae/project/packages/lib/src/pages/LoginPage/index.tsx`

**Props:**
- `ignoreAuthCheck?: boolean` - Skip auth check for testing

**Features:**
- Username/password form with LeafyGreen TextInput
- Redirects to "/" or location state referrer if already authenticated
- Shows FullPageLoad while checking auth
- Green-themed form styling

1→export enum TaskStatus {
     2→  Inactive = "inactive",
     3→
     4→  // TaskUnstarted is assigned to a display task after cleaning up one of
     5→  // its execution tasks. This indicates that the display task is
     6→  // pending a rerun
     7→  Unstarted = "unstarted",
     8→
     9→  // TaskUndispatched indicates either
    10→  //  1. a task is not scheduled to run (when Task.Activated == false)
    11→  //  2. a task is scheduled to run (when Task.Activated == true)
    12→  Undispatched = "undispatched",
    13→  WillRun = "will-run",
    14→  Unscheduled = "unscheduled",
    15→
    16→  // TaskStarted indicates a task is currently running
    17→  Started = "started",
    18→
    19→  // TaskDispatched indicates that an agent has received the task, but
    20→  // the agent has not yet told Evergreen that it's running the task
    21→  Dispatched = "dispatched",
    22→
    23→  // This is a temporary status which is the same as undispatched, but
    24→  // with the additional info that it's waiting for a dependency
    25→  Pending = "pending",
    26→
    27→  // The statuses below indicate that a task has finished.
    28→  Succeeded = "success",
    29→
    30→  // These statuses indicate that the task failed, and it is likely a problem

    35→
    36→  // These statuses indicate that the task failed, and it is likely a problem

Key observations:

3. Several components need react-router-dom (Pagination uses usePagination which uses useQueryParam which uses useLocation/useNavigate)

5. No MSW needed since components don't fetch data directly (AuthProvider does, but we don't need it for most stories)


6. LoginPage requires both AuthProvider and react-router-dom

My approach is to update preview.tsx with global styles and a MemoryRouter decorator for router-dependent components, then write stories covering different states for Accordion, TaskStatusBadge, TestStatusBadge, Pagination, and Page components.

I'm realizing Pagination depends on useQueryParam which relies on react-router-dom, so I'll need the MemoryRouter wrapper. For Pagination specifically, I can either wrap everything in MemoryRouter or pass an `onChange` prop to bypass the `usePagination` hook, though the hook still gets called regardless.

Since `usePagination` is always invoked, MemoryRouter is necessary. content.thinking:
The issue is `Buffer is not defined` - this is a browser environment issue with `@leafygreen-ui/tokens` importing `@emotion/server` which uses `html-tokenize` which uses `buffer-from`. This happens because the preview imports `globalStyles` which imports from `@leafygreen-ui/tokens`.

The error is:
```
Caused by: ReferenceError: Buffer is not defined
 ❯ buffer-from/index.js
 ❯ html-tokenize/index.js
 ❯ @emotion/server/create-instance/dist/emotion-server-create-instance.browser.esm.js
```

The dependency chain shows that during Vite's optimization phase, `@leafygreen-ui/tokens` pulls in `@emotion/server`, which transitively requires `Buffer` through `html-tokenize` and `buffer-from`. I need to either polyfill `Buffer` or restructure how the global styles are imported to avoid this chain.

Looking at the error more carefully, it's affecting all tests including the pre-existing demo stories, which suggests this is a pre-existing issue with the package dependencies rather than something I introduced. The error message hints at adding dependencies to `optimizeDeps.include` as a potential fix, but the core problem is that `Buffer` isn't available in the browser environment. I should verify whether the demo stories were passing before my changes to confirm this isn't something I broke.

Actually, I see the issue now - my `globalStyles` import is pulling in `@leafygreen-ui/tokens`, which gets pre-bundled by Storybook/Vitest and that's where the `Buffer` error occurs. The error stack traces show different sources depending on the test, but they all trace back to the tokens package being bundled incorrectly for the browser environment.

Let me try inlining the global styles directly in preview.tsx instead of importing them, which should bypass the tokens dependency issue entirely. Or I should first verify whether this problem existed before my changes or if I introduced it by adding the globalStyles import.

content.text: The `Buffer is not defined` error is caused by `@leafygreen-ui/tokens` being imported in the browser. Let me fix the preview to inline the global styles instead.

content.thinking:
So 4 passed and 9 failed with `Buffer is not defined`. The 4 that passed are: FullPageLoad (no emotion/styled), Accordion (uses emotion/styled but maybe cached), and the 3 demo stories in src/stories/.

Wait - looking more carefully at the results, the ones that passed are FullPageLoad and the 3 demo stories (Button, Header, Page). But the demo stories use their own custom components (not leafygreen). All my stories that use LeafyGreen components fail with the Buffer error.

The issue is that `@emotion/server` has a dependency on `html-tokenize` -> `buffer-from` which needs `Buffer` in browser. This is a known issue with @emotion in browser environments.


I need to polyfill Buffer to fix this. I could add it through the vitest config with a global definition, or use vite's `resolve.alias` to redirect the problematic `buffer-from` module to a browser-compatible alternative.

Looking at the actual error chain, the issue is that `@emotion/server` is being pulled in transitively through LeafyGreen, which then tries to import `html-tokenize`, which depends on `buffer-from` that needs the Buffer global. The cleanest solution is to either exclude `@emotion/server` from optimization or provide a Buffer shim through a setup file. I'm going in circles here - the real issue is that `@emotion/server` shouldn't be loaded in the browser at all, so I should alias it to an empty module or exclude it from the bundle entirely rather than trying to polyfill Buffer.

Let me just clear the cache and run the tests again to see if this is a stale build problem.

content.text: The `Buffer is not defined` error comes from `@emotion/server` being transitively imported in the browser. I need to provide a Buffer polyfill. Let me fix this in the vitest config.

content.thinking:
Progress! The Buffer error is gone now, but there's a new issue: `TypeError: Right-hand side of 'instanceof' is not callable` - probably from the `events.EventEmitter` externalization. The `html-tokenize` module uses Node.js `events` module which is being externalized by Vite for browser compatibility.

The issue is that `@emotion/server` -> `html-tokenize` uses Node.js streams which depend on `events`. This entire module chain is server-side only and shouldn't be in the browser.

Let me try a different approach - instead of polyfilling, let me alias `@emotion/server` to an empty module since it's not needed for browser rendering. 

That's much cleaner.

content.text: Buffer is fixed but now `events.EventEmitter` is externalized. The root cause is `@emotion/server` shouldn't be in the browser at all. Let me alias it away.

content.text: Also remove the buffer polyfill setup file since we won't need it with the alias approach.

Now I can see the issues clearly:

1. **Pagination**: Uses `data-cy` not `data-testid` - need `getByTestId` -> use `[data-cy]` selector
2. **Accordion CssCheck**: Multiple elements match - need to be more specific
3. **Popconfirm**: Elements not visible (tooltip not rendering visibly) and uses `aria-disabled` instead of `disabled`

Let me fix all three.

content.thinking:
The Accordion and Pagination stories pass now. But Popconfirm still fails - the tooltip isn't rendering. The output shows "No Preview" and "Sorry, but you either have no stories" - which suggests the canvas is empty/blank when the story renders. The tooltip uses `triggerEvent="click"` and renders via a portal.

The issue is that `Popconfirm` wraps `Tooltip`, and the Tooltip component portals content to `document.body`. But the `open` state is initially `false`, and when we click the button, the popover might not be opening correctly because of how React hooks work in stories with `render()` functions.
 
So the real problem is that the tooltip element never actually gets added to the DOM - when I query for it with `querySelector('[data-lgid="lg-tooltip"]')`, it returns null. The `open` prop is controlled, and clicking the trigger should call `setOpen(true)`, but something's preventing the tooltip from actually rendering into the portal. I see the structure now—the Popconfirm wraps its content (the confirmation message and buttons) inside the Tooltip, while the actual trigger button lives outside and controls the `open` state through the ref. The problem is likely that `buttonRef.current` is null during the initial render, so `refEl` becomes undefined, which might cause the Tooltip to not position itself correctly or handle the reference element properly.

Looking at the LeafyGreen Tooltip API, it expects `refEl` to be either a React ref or an actual DOM element, but passing undefined on first render could be causing issues. The real problem might be that the tooltip is rendering in a portal outside the visible canvas, or the Storybook iframe itself is interfering with how the tooltip displays. Let me reconsider the approach—maybe I need to ensure the ref is properly available before the Popconfirm tries to use it, or check if there's something about how Storybook handles portals that's preventing the tooltip from appearing. The Tooltip component needs either a `trigger` prop or a combination of `refEl` and controlled `open` state, but passing `refEl={buttonRef.current ?? undefined}` causes timing issues since the ref might be null on initial render while the open state changes. I should try a simpler rendering approach that doesn't rely on this ref timing problem.

content.thinking:
The type errors are all about "Property 'args' is missing" - this happens when stories use `render` without providing `args`. This is because the component has required props, and Storybook's strict typing requires `args` when there are required props on the component.

The fix is to either:
1. Provide empty/minimal `args` alongside the `render` function
2. Use `@ts-expect-error` comments (not ideal)
3. Change the meta to not have a `component` (not ideal)

The cleanest approach is to provide `args` with the required props alongside `render`.

Also there's a type error in Popconfirm: `Property 'current' is missing in type 'HTMLButtonElement' but required in type 'RefObject<HTMLElement>'`. 

The `refEl` prop expects a `RefObject<HTMLElement>` but we're passing an `HTMLButtonElement`, so I need to adjust the type casting to match what the component expects.

Do not skip steps or │ attempt to do them out of order. │ Your goal is to make Storybook fully functional in this project by │ analyzing the codebase, │ configuring the preview with the right decorators, and writing stories │ for some components. │ The end state should be a Storybook where any component — from a small │ button to a full page — can be added without story-specific │ workarounds. All necessary providers, CSS, browser state, and network │ mocks should live in the shared preview so that just rendering the │ component in the story is enough. │ After each created story, run Vitest to verify it renders. │ If the test fails, read the error, fix the issue, and re-run until it │ passes before moving on. │ - Copy real patterns from the codebase │ - Keep the app code unchanged │ - Put the default setup in `.storybook/preview.tsx` │ - Keep app mocking and runtime setup in `.storybook/preview.tsx`, not │ in the stories │ ### Storybook Documentation Reference │ Use the following references to look up Storybook APIs, concepts, or │ examples: │ - Full docs index: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/llms.txthttps://storybook.js.org/llms.txt]8;; │ - See code snippets only with codeOnly=true param e.g. │ ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories.md?renderer=react&language=ts&codeOnly=truehttps://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories.md?renderer=react&language=ts&codeOnly=true]8;; │ Key documentation pages for this task: │ - Writing stories: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories.md?renderer=react&language=tshttps://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories...]8;; │ - Decorators: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories/decorators.md?renderer=react&language=tshttps://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories/deco...]8;; │ - Args: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories/args.md?renderer=react&language=tshttps://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories/args.md?re...]8;; │ - Play functions: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories/play-function.md?renderer=react&language=tshttps://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories/...]8;; │ - Vitest integration: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-tests/vitest-plugin.md?renderer=react&language=tshttps://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-test...]8;; │ Fetch these URLs directly when you need guidance on Storybook APIs or │ patterns. │ ### Step 1: Analyze the codebase │ Read enough of the app to understand the full runtime environment │ before writing any stories. │ Do not stop at `main.tsx` or `App.tsx`. │ Follow imports into providers, pages, hooks, and shared components │ until you know: │ - which providers exist │ - which CSS files are injected │ - which queries fetch data │ - which browser-state reads happen │ - which portals and portal roots exist │ - which pages and components show the real usage patterns │ Example of what to copy: │ ```tsx │ // src/main.tsx │ import "./index.css"; │ import App from "./App"; │ import { SessionProvider } from "./contexts/SessionContext"; │ createRoot(document.getElementById("root")!).render( │ │ │ , │ ); │ ``` │ That means Storybook should copy: │ - the `index.css` import │ - the `SessionProvider` │ - the same provider order │ Example of tracing the app deeper: │ ```tsx │ // src/App.tsx │ function App() { │ const { products, loadMoreProducts } = useProducts(); │ const { currentUser, signOut } = useSession(); │ // ... │ } │ ``` │ ```ts │ // src/hooks/useProducts.ts │ const response = await fetch(apiBaseUrl + "/products?page=1"); │ ``` │ ```ts │ // src/hooks/useTheme.ts │ const savedTheme = localStorage.getItem("theme"); │ ``` │ That means the default Storybook setup should discover and prepare: │ - provider state │ - MSW handlers for queries │ - browser-state values that are actually read during render │ ### Step 2: Build one default app environment in preview │ Set up Storybook once so most stories work without story-specific │ setup. │ Start with the smallest faithful environment: │ - the real provider tree │ - the real root CSS │ - seeded browser state if the app reads it during render │ - MSW for network/data queries │ It is fine to seed browser state such as `localStorage`, │ `sessionStorage`, and cookies when the app reads them during render. │ Seed only the specific app-owned keys and values you need. │ Do not clear all `localStorage`, `sessionStorage`, or cookies, and do │ not reset Storybook's own state. │ Do not mock or redefine the browser runtime itself. │ The stories run in Vitest browser mode, so the real browser │ environment should already exist. │ ```tsx │ // .storybook/preview.tsx │ import '../src/index.css'; // import global styles │ import MockDate from 'mockdate'; │ import { definePreview } from 'storybook/preview'; │ import { SessionProvider } from '../src/contexts/SessionContext'; │ export default definePreview({ │ decorators: [ │ (Story) => ( │ │ │ │ ), │ ], │ async beforeEach() { │ localStorage.setItem('theme', 'dark'); │ localStorage.setItem('sidebar:open', 'true'); │ MockDate.set('2024-04-01T12:00:00Z'); │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ Use this same idea for: │ - providers │ - root CSS │ - browser state │ - dates, and if the app logic depends on them during render then │ always use `mockdate` │ Example with the `mockdate` package: │ ```tsx │ import MockDate from 'mockdate'; │ import { definePreview } from 'storybook/preview'; │ export default definePreview({ │ async beforeEach() { │ MockDate.set('2024-04-01T12:00:00Z'); │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ ### Step 3: Support portals with preview-body.html │ If the app uses portals, copy that setup into Storybook too. │ Look for patterns like: │ - `createPortal(...)` │ - modal, dialog, drawer, popover, tooltip, toast, or dropdown portal │ components │ - hard-coded roots such as `#portal-root`, `#modal-root`, │ `#drawer-root`, or `#toast-root` │ Example of what to copy: │ ```tsx │ // real component │ return createPortal(, │ document.getElementById("portal-root")!); │ ``` │ That means Storybook should create the same portal root in │ `.storybook/preview-body.html`: │ ```html │ │
│ ``` │ If the app uses multiple portal roots, create all of them there: │ ```html │ │
│
│
│ ``` │ If a library portals directly to `document.body`, do not add extra │ roots for it. │ Make sure the copied page shell, CSS, and layout still allow overlays, │ fixed positioning, and z-index stacking to render correctly. │ ### Step 4: Mock side effects globally │ All network/data queries should be handled by the default Storybook │ environment. │ - Always use `msw-storybook-addon` for query mocking. │ - If you introduce MSW, run `npx msw init ./public --save` to create │ the worker file. │ - Make sure Storybook serves `./public` as a static dir so │ `mockServiceWorker.js` is available. │ - Do not mock `fetch` directly. │ - Network/data queries should return deterministic mock data. │ - If you need to change dependencies, first check the lockfile and use │ that package manager for the change. │ Example of copying a real fetch pattern into shared handlers: │ ```ts │ // real app hook │ const response = await fetch( │ apiBaseUrl + │ "/products?" + │ new URLSearchParams({ │ page: "1", │ sort: "featured", │ }), │ ); │ ``` │ ```ts │ // .storybook/msw-handlers.ts │ import { http, HttpResponse } from "msw"; │ export const mswHandlers = { │ products: [ │ http.get("]8;;https://api.example.com/products",https://api.example.com/products",]8;; │ () => │ HttpResponse.json({ │ items: [ │ { │ id: "product-1", │ name: "Example product", │ description: "Mock product description", │ imageUrl: │ "]8;;https://images.example.com/product.jpg",https://images.example.com/product.jpg",]8;; │ price: 42, │ }, │ ], │ }), │ ), │ ], │ }; │ ``` │ ```tsx │ // .storybook/preview.tsx │ import { definePreview } from 'storybook/preview'; │ import { initialize, mswLoader } from 'msw-storybook-addon'; │ import { mswHandlers } from './msw-handlers'; │ initialize({ │ onUnhandledRequest: 'bypass', │ }); │ export default definePreview({ │ loaders: [mswLoader], │ parameters: { │ msw: { │ handlers: mswHandlers, │ }, │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ ```ts │ // .storybook/main.ts │ import type { StorybookConfig } from "@storybook/react-vite"; │ const config: StorybookConfig = { │ staticDirs: ["../public"], │ }; │ export default config; │ ``` │ Keep these mocks global. │ Do not put fetch mocks in individual stories. │ Only add handlers for requests that the shared preview setup or the │ stories actually use. │ Do not add catch-all handlers that can hide unrelated failures. │ If the defaults are not enough, improve the shared default setup │ instead. │ Seed browser state when needed, but do not mock `window`, `document`, │ `navigator`, observers, or similar runtime APIs. │ The only exception is `mockdate` when date-based rendering exists. │ ### Step 5: Write stories │ Try to find around 10 good candidate components for story files. │ Write colocated stories for top-level components, from low-level │ reusable components up to page components. │ Write up to 10 story files, or fewer only if the codebase clearly has │ fewer meaningful targets. │ The stories should use JSX copied from real usage patterns in: │ - pages │ - app shells │ - routes │ - tests │ - existing feature code │ As a rule of thumb, each story file should have around 3 story exports │ when the component or page has enough meaningful states. │ It can have more when the real usage supports it, up to 10 story │ exports in one file. │ Always show all imports explicitly in story and preview files. │ Do not rely on omitted or implied imports in examples or generated │ code. │ #### Story tags │ Every story meta must include the `ai-generated` tag to identify │ AI-created stories: │ ```tsx │ import preview from '#.storybook/preview'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { SomeComponent } from './SomeComponent'; │ const meta = preview.meta({ │ component: SomeComponent, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ }); │ export const Default = meta.story({ │ render: () => , │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button')).toBeVisible(); │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ If a story could not be fully fixed after the self-healing loop (the │ test still fails │ or the rendering is incomplete), add the `needs-work` tag alongside │ `ai-generated`: │ ```ts │ const meta = preview.meta({ │ component: SomeComponent, │ tags: ['ai-generated', 'needs-work'], │ }); │ ``` │ #### Args vs render │ For simple components where props drive the state, prefer `args` │ stories — no `render` function needed: │ ```tsx │ import preview from '#.storybook/preview'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { Button } from './Button'; │ const meta = preview.meta({ │ component: Button, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ }); │ export const Primary = meta.story({ │ args: { │ variant: 'primary', │ children: 'Save', │ }, │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button', { name: /save/i │ })).toBeVisible(); │ }, │ }); │ export const Disabled = meta.story({ │ args: { │ variant: 'primary', │ disabled: true, │ children: 'Save', │ }, │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button')).toBeDisabled(); │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ Use `render` when the story needs composition — wrapping the component │ in layout, combining multiple components, or passing children as JSX: │ ```tsx │ import preview from '#.storybook/preview'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { Button } from './Button'; │ import { Card } from './Card'; │ const meta = preview.meta({ │ component: Button, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ }); │ export const InsideCard = meta.story({ │ render: () => ( │ │ │ │ ), │ play: async ({ canvas, userEvent }) => { │ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button', { name: /save/i │ })).toBeVisible(); │ await userEvent.click(canvas.getByRole('button', { name: /save/i })); │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ Keep app mocking and runtime setup in preview, not in the stories. │ Do not build large story-specific harnesses. │ Do not write story files for subcomponents, hooks, contexts, or │ helpers. │ Do not create new application components. │ Do not add a custom `title`. │ Do not stop after only a few easy targets if the codebase has more │ meaningful components or pages available. │ ### Step 6: Write a play function for every story │ Every named story export must have a `play` function. │ The `play` function is not optional, even for simple stories. │ The purpose of the `play` function is to prove that the story actually │ works in the copied Storybook environment: │ - the story renders something real and non-empty │ - the decorators provide the needed context │ - the CSS is applied well enough for the intended state to be visible │ - the MSW mocks or seeded browser state are actually being used │ - important interactions, async loading states, and portals behave │ correctly │ Use `play` functions to verify behavior, not just to click around. │ A story without assertions is incomplete. │ Use tools from `storybook/test` such as: │ - `expect` │ - `waitFor` │ Prefer `canvas` and `userEvent` from the `play` context. │ Do not destructure `canvasElement` just to create `const canvas = │ within(canvasElement)`. │ Do not import `userEvent` from `storybook/test`; use `userEvent` from │ the `play` context instead. │ Only use `canvasElement.ownerDocument` when you need to query outside │ the canvas, such as for portals. │ Example: │ ```tsx │ import type { StoryObj } from "@storybook/react-vite"; │ export const FilledForm: Story = { │ play: async ({ canvas, userEvent }) => { │ const emailInput = canvas.getByLabelText("email", { │ selector: "input", │ }); │ await userEvent.type(emailInput, "example-email@email.com", { │ delay: 100, │ }); │ const passwordInput = canvas.getByLabelText("password", { │ selector: "input", │ }); │ await userEvent.type(passwordInput, "ExamplePassword", { │ delay: 100, │ }); │ const submitButton = canvas.getByRole("button"); │ await userEvent.click(submitButton); │ }, │ }; │ ``` │ The assertions should match the real pattern you copied: │ - for provider-backed stories, assert the provider-dependent UI │ appears correctly │ - for mocked-data stories, wait for the mocked data to appear and │ assert on it │ - for CSS-sensitive states, assert on visibility, text layout, │ class-driven states, or meaningful computed styles │ - for routing or navigation stories, assert the routed state or │ navigation outcome │ - for portal stories, query from `canvasElement.ownerDocument` when │ the UI renders outside the canvas │ Examples of useful checks: │ - a themed button has the expected label and is visibly enabled or │ disabled │ - a modal opened through a decorator or provider is visible in the │ portal root │ - mocked API data appears in the page instead of a loading spinner │ forever │ - a selected tab actually shows the selected panel │ - a toast, alert, or badge has the expected accessible text and visual │ state │ - a CSS class or computed style confirms the real state that matters │ ### Step 7: Prove CSS is loaded in exactly one story named `CssCheck` │ In exactly one story, named `CssCheck`, assert a component-specific │ computed style. `toBeVisible` passes on an unstyled component; a │ concrete style value proves the shared preview loaded the app's CSS. │ Pick a visually distinctive component, read a styling value from its │ source, and assert it with `getComputedStyle`: │ ```tsx │ export const CssCheck: Story = { │ args: { children: "Submit" }, │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ const button = canvas.getByRole("button", { name: /submit/i }); │ // PrimaryButton uses bg-blue-600 — fails if Tailwind / global CSS did │ not load. │ await expect(getComputedStyle(button).backgroundColor).toBe("rgb(37, │ 99, 235)"); │ }, │ }; │ ``` │ ### Step 8: Cover the patterns you found │ Write stories for the real patterns in the codebase, for example: │ - a low-level reusable component in real JSX usage │ - a provider-backed component │ - a browser-state-backed component │ - a fetched-data component │ - a real page component │ Use `App.tsx` to inspect the real provider tree and usage patterns, │ but do not make a story for `App` when the codebase has actual page │ components. │ Example page story: │ ```tsx │ import preview from '#.storybook/preview'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { ProductPage } from './ProductPage'; │ const meta = preview.meta({ │ component: ProductPage, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ }); │ export const Default = meta.story({ │ render: () => , │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect( │ canvas.getByRole('heading', { name: /products/i }), │ ).toBeVisible(); │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ ### Step 9: Verify both rendering and types │ As you work, verify the stories with Vitest: │ ```bash │ npx vitest --project storybook │ ``` │ Also verify types so you catch missing required props, broken imports, │ and preview typing issues. Run the same TypeScript command the project │ itself uses. │ ```bash │ │ ``` │ After verification passes, review every changed file and remove │ anything that is not needed for the final solution, especially debug │ fixes, overly broad mocks, unnecessary dependencies, and eval │ artifacts. │ Keep iterating until: │ - every story you wrote passes │ - every story you wrote has a meaningful passing `play` function │ - the changed stories and preview setup pass the project's real │ TypeScript check │ - the rendered output looks sensible │ - the default global mocked environment is strong enough that stories │ do not need manual fetch overrides │ - stories no longer fail because the shared preview setup and story │ JSX are fixed │ - all passing stories have `tags: ['ai-generated']` in their meta │ - any stories that still need work have `tags: ['ai-generated', │ 'needs-work']` in their meta
